---
title: "Exam 5A: Computability and Abstract Algebra"
subtitle: "Lessons 67--72 Comprehensive Assessment"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.8in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{mathtools}
      \usepackage{float}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time Allowed} & \~{}3 hours \\
\textbf{Total Points} & 100 \\
\textbf{Materials} & Pencil, paper. No calculator needed. \\
\textbf{Format} & 10 questions, 10 pts each. Show all work. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{0.8em}

> *Each question synthesizes concepts across multiple lessons. Partial credit is generous for correct reasoning with arithmetic errors.*

\newpage

## Q1 (10 pts) --- Turing Machines, Decidability, and the Halting Problem

A colleague proposes building a "universal alignment checker" --- a program $\texttt{SAFE}(M, x)$ that takes any Turing machine $M$ and input $x$, and decides whether $M$ on input $x$ ever produces an output violating a fixed safety specification $\varphi$.

**(a)** Define a Turing machine formally: state set $Q$, tape alphabet $\Gamma$, transition function $\delta$, start state $q_0$, accept/reject states. Explain the Church--Turing thesis and why it matters for the universality claim above.

**(b)** State the halting problem precisely. Prove it is undecidable by constructing the diagonalisation argument: assume a decider $H(M, x)$ exists, build the adversary machine $D$, and derive a contradiction from $D(D)$.

**(c)** Using a reduction from the halting problem, prove that $\texttt{SAFE}(M, x)$ is undecidable. *(Hint: given an instance of the halting problem $\langle M, x \rangle$, construct a machine $M'$ that runs $M$ on $x$ and, if $M$ halts, produces an output violating $\varphi$.)*

**(d)** Rice's theorem generalises the above. State it, and explain why "Does this neural network implement a deceptive strategy?" is undecidable for arbitrary programs. In 2--3 sentences, argue why this does *not* mean partial safety verification is impossible.

---

## Q2 (10 pts) --- Complexity Classes and Polynomial Reductions

**(a)** Define the classes $\mathsf{P}$, $\mathsf{NP}$, and $\mathsf{NP}$-complete. State the relationship $\mathsf{P} \subseteq \mathsf{NP}$ and explain why the question $\mathsf{P} \stackrel{?}{=} \mathsf{NP}$ is considered the most important open problem in theoretical computer science.

**(b)** The Boolean satisfiability problem ($\textsc{SAT}$) asks: given a propositional formula $\phi$ in conjunctive normal form, does there exist an assignment of truth values making $\phi$ true? Classify $\textsc{SAT}$ among the complexity classes above. State the Cook--Levin theorem.

**(c)** Define polynomial-time reduction $A \leq_p B$. Show that if $B \in \mathsf{P}$ and $A \leq_p B$, then $A \in \mathsf{P}$. Explain in 2--3 sentences why this makes $\mathsf{NP}$-complete problems the "hardest" problems in $\mathsf{NP}$.

**(d)** An alignment researcher wants to verify that a ReLU network $f: \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ never outputs $1$ on any input (i.e., $f(x) = 0$ for all $x$). Explain why this verification problem is $\mathsf{coNP}$-hard by sketching a reduction from $\textsc{TAUT}$ (the tautology problem). What does this imply for scalable safety certification of large networks?

---

## Q3 (10 pts) --- Kolmogorov Complexity, Incompressibility, and Learning

Let $K(x)$ denote the Kolmogorov complexity of a binary string $x$ relative to a fixed universal Turing machine $U$.

**(a)** Define $K(x)$ precisely. State the invariance theorem: for any two universal machines $U_1, U_2$, there exists a constant $c$ such that $|K_{U_1}(x) - K_{U_2}(x)| \leq c$ for all $x$. Why does this justify speaking of "the" Kolmogorov complexity up to a constant?

**(b)** Prove that $K$ is uncomputable. *(Hint: assume a computable $K$; consider the program "print the first string $x$ with $K(x) > n$" and derive a contradiction by bounding its length.)*

**(c)** A string $x$ of length $n$ is called *incompressible* if $K(x) \geq n$. Show by a counting argument that at least half of all binary strings of length $n$ satisfy $K(x) \geq n - 1$. What does this tell us about the "typicality" of random-looking strings?

**(d)** Solomonoff induction assigns prior probability $P(H) = 2^{-K(H)}$ to hypothesis $H$. Explain how this implements Occam's razor. Connect to practical model selection: how does the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle approximate Kolmogorov complexity, and what role does the RLCT from singular learning theory play as a refined complexity measure?

---

## Q4 (10 pts) --- Group Axioms, Subgroups, and Homomorphisms

**(a)** State the four group axioms. Verify that $(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}, +)$, the integers modulo 6 under addition, is a group by checking each axiom explicitly.

**(b)** Find all subgroups of $(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}, +)$. For each subgroup $H$, list its elements and verify $|H|$ divides $|\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}| = 6$ (Lagrange's theorem).

**(c)** Define a group homomorphism $\varphi: G \to G'$. Consider $\varphi: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ defined by $\varphi(n) = n \bmod 6$. Verify $\varphi$ is a homomorphism. Compute $\ker(\varphi)$ and identify the quotient group $\mathbb{Z}/\ker(\varphi)$. State the first isomorphism theorem and confirm it holds here.

**(d)** Explain why $(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}, +) \cong (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}) \times (\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z})$ but $(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}, +) \not\cong (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}) \times (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})$. *(Hint: find an element of order 4 in one but not the other.)*

---

## Q5 (10 pts) --- Cyclic Groups, Symmetric Groups, and Permutation Composition

**(a)** Define a cyclic group. Show that $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ is cyclic by finding a generator. State the classification theorem: every cyclic group of order $n$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$.

**(b)** The symmetric group $S_3$ consists of all permutations of $\{1, 2, 3\}$. List all 6 elements in cycle notation. Identify the identity, all transpositions, and all 3-cycles. Determine which elements are even and which are odd.

**(c)** Compute the following products in $S_3$ (apply right-to-left):

$$\sigma_1 = (1\;2\;3)(1\;2), \qquad \sigma_2 = (1\;2)(1\;2\;3), \qquad \sigma_3 = (1\;2\;3)^2$$

Is $S_3$ abelian? Justify using your computations.

**(d)** A neural network with $n$ hidden neurons in a layer has weight-space symmetry group $S_n$ (permuting neurons and corresponding weights yields the same function). For $n = 3$, how many equivalent weight-space points represent the same function? For general $n$, express this number. A network with two hidden layers of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$ has symmetry group $S_{n_1} \times S_{n_2}$. How many equivalent configurations exist?

---

## Q6 (10 pts) --- Rings, Ideals, and Polynomial Rings

**(a)** State the ring axioms (for a commutative ring with unity). What additional axiom distinguishes a field from a ring? Give one example of a field and one example of a ring that is not a field.

**(b)** Consider the polynomial ring $\mathbb{R}[x]$. Explain why it is a ring but not a field. Define an ideal $I$ of a ring $R$, and show that $I = (x^2 + 1) = \{f(x)(x^2 + 1) : f(x) \in \mathbb{R}[x]\}$ is an ideal of $\mathbb{R}[x]$.

**(c)** The quotient ring $\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2 + 1)$ consists of equivalence classes of polynomials modulo $x^2 + 1$. Show that in this ring, $x^2 = -1$. Explain why every element can be written as $a + bx$ with $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. What familiar mathematical object is $\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2 + 1)$ isomorphic to?

**(d)** Near a singularity $w_0$ in the loss landscape, the loss function can be expanded as a polynomial $L(w) \approx \sum c_\alpha (w - w_0)^\alpha$. The partial derivatives $\partial L / \partial w_i$ generate an ideal $J$ in the local polynomial ring, and the geometry of the zero set $V(J)$ determines the RLCT. In 2--3 sentences, explain why polynomial rings and ideal theory provide the natural algebraic framework for studying singularities in learning theory.

---

## Q7 (10 pts) --- Field Extensions and Algebraic Structure

**(a)** Define a field extension $F \subseteq E$. The degree of the extension is $[E : F] = \dim_F E$ (the dimension of $E$ as a vector space over $F$). Compute $[\mathbb{C} : \mathbb{R}]$ by finding a basis for $\mathbb{C}$ over $\mathbb{R}$.

**(b)** Consider $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}) = \{a + b\sqrt{2} : a, b \in \mathbb{Q}\}$. Show this is a field by verifying that the multiplicative inverse of $a + b\sqrt{2}$ (for $a + b\sqrt{2} \neq 0$) lies in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$. Compute $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}) : \mathbb{Q}]$.

**(c)** The tower law states: if $F \subseteq K \subseteq E$, then $[E : F] = [E : K]\,[K : F]$. Use this to compute $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}) : \mathbb{Q}]$, given that $\sqrt{3} \notin \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$.

**(d)** Galois theory connects field extensions to group theory: the automorphisms of $E$ that fix $F$ form a group $\operatorname{Gal}(E/F)$. For the extension $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})/\mathbb{Q}$, find $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})/\mathbb{Q})$ by determining all field automorphisms that fix $\mathbb{Q}$. What group is this isomorphic to?

---

## Q8 (10 pts) --- Group Actions, Orbits, and Stabilisers

Let $G$ be a group acting on a set $X$.

**(a)** Define a group action formally (two axioms). Define the orbit $\operatorname{Orb}(x)$ and stabiliser $\operatorname{Stab}(x)$ of a point $x \in X$. State the orbit--stabiliser theorem: $|G| = |\operatorname{Orb}(x)| \cdot |\operatorname{Stab}(x)|$.

**(b)** The group $S_3$ acts on the weight space of a 3-hidden-unit neural network by permuting neurons. Consider three scenarios:

- *Configuration A:* All three neurons have distinct weights.
- *Configuration B:* Exactly two neurons share the same weights; the third differs.
- *Configuration C:* All three neurons have identical weights.

For each configuration, compute $|\operatorname{Orb}|$ and $|\operatorname{Stab}|$, and verify the orbit--stabiliser theorem.

**(c)** Explain in 2--3 sentences why configurations with large stabilisers correspond to more singular points in the loss landscape. How does the size of the stabiliser relate to the number of "flat directions" in parameter space?

**(d)** A molecule has rotational symmetry group $\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$ (quarter-turn rotations). It can be coloured using 2 colours. Using Burnside's lemma,

$$|\text{distinct colourings}| = \frac{1}{|G|} \sum_{g \in G} |X^g|$$

compute the number of distinct colourings up to rotational symmetry, where $X^g$ denotes the set of colourings fixed by $g$. Assume the molecule has 4 sites arranged in a cycle.

---

## Q9 (10 pts) --- Burnside's Lemma and Symmetry Applications

A necklace has 4 beads arranged in a circle, and each bead can be one of 3 colours: red, green, or blue.

**(a)** Without accounting for symmetry, how many total colourings exist?

**(b)** The symmetry group of the necklace is the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z} = \{e, r, r^2, r^3\}$ where $r$ is a $90°$ clockwise rotation. For each group element $g$, compute $|X^g|$, the number of colourings fixed by $g$:

- $g = e$ (identity):
- $g = r$ ($90°$ rotation):
- $g = r^2$ ($180°$ rotation):
- $g = r^3$ ($270°$ rotation):

**(c)** Apply Burnside's lemma to compute the number of distinct necklaces up to rotation.

**(d)** Now suppose the necklace can also be flipped (reflected), so the symmetry group is the dihedral group $D_4$ of order 8. The additional elements are two reflections through beads and two reflections through midpoints of edges. Compute $|X^g|$ for each of the 4 new elements and use Burnside's lemma to find the number of distinct necklaces under the full $D_4$ symmetry. Explain in 1--2 sentences why accounting for more symmetry reduces the count.

---

## Q10 (10 pts) --- Synthesis: Computability Meets Algebra

**(a)** A "universal Turing machine" $U$ can simulate any Turing machine $M$ given its description $\langle M \rangle$. Similarly, a "universal group" would contain every finite group as a subgroup. Cayley's theorem states that every group $G$ is isomorphic to a subgroup of $S_{|G|}$. Explain why Cayley's theorem is the algebraic analogue of universality in computation. Is $S_n$ "universal" for groups of order $\leq n!$?

**(b)** In a neural network with $n$ hidden neurons, the weight-space symmetry group is $S_n$. The orbit of a generic weight vector under $S_n$ has size $n!$, and the loss function is invariant on orbits. Define the quotient space $W / S_n$ (the space of orbits). Explain why optimising on $W / S_n$ would eliminate permutation-equivalent critical points, and state one reason this is difficult in practice.

**(c)** The Kolmogorov complexity $K(G)$ of a finite group $G$ can be defined as the length of the shortest program outputting the multiplication table of $G$. Argue informally that $K(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) = O(\log n)$ while $K(S_n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. What does this say about which groups are "simple" in an information-theoretic sense?

**(d)** Bring together the threads of this exam. A neural network's loss landscape has singularities arising from algebraic symmetry ($S_n$ group actions, L70/L72); the geometry of these singularities is studied via polynomial rings and ideals (L71); the network's true complexity is measured by the RLCT rather than parameter count; and the fundamental limits of verifying network behaviour are set by undecidability (L67) and computational complexity (L68). In 4--5 sentences, explain how these mathematical frameworks collectively inform the study of AI alignment.
